expanded_description: Understand the three major acid-base theories—Arrhenius, Brønsted-Lowry, and Lewis—and how each expands our ability to classify and predict acid-base behavior across aqueous and non-aqueous systems.

core_concept.principle: Acid-base theories provide progressively broader frameworks for identifying proton or electron-pair transfer reactions: Arrhenius (H⁺/OH⁻ in water), Brønsted-Lowry (proton donors/acceptors), and Lewis (electron-pair acceptors/donors).
core_concept.explanation: The Arrhenius theory (1884) defines acids as substances that produce $\text{H}^+$ ions and bases as substances that produce $\text{OH}^-$ ions in aqueous solution. The Brønsted-Lowry theory (1923) generalizes this to any proton ($\text{H}^+$) transfer reaction, introducing conjugate acid-base pairs and explaining behavior in non-aqueous solvents. The Lewis theory (1923) further expands the definition to electron-pair transfer, encompassing reactions without protons entirely, such as $\text{BF}_3 + \text{:NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{F}_3\text{B:NH}_3$. Each theory includes the previous one while extending applicability to broader chemical contexts.

image.alt: Venn diagram showing how Lewis theory encompasses Brønsted-Lowry, which encompasses Arrhenius
image.caption: Each successive theory includes the previous while expanding the scope of acid-base reactions

sections.introduction: Proposed by Svante Arrhenius in 1884, this was the first quantitative theory of acids and bases, linking their behavior to ionic dissociation in water. While limited to aqueous solutions, it established the foundation for understanding electrolyte behavior and pH.

key_concept.definition: Arrhenius Acid: A substance that dissociates in water to produce hydrogen ions ($\text{H}^+$); Arrhenius Base: A substance that dissociates in water to produce hydroxide ions ($\text{OH}^-$)
key_concept.context: This theory explains conductivity of acid/base solutions and neutralization as $\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$

image.alt: Diagram showing HCl and NaOH dissociating in water
image.caption: Arrhenius acids and bases dissociate into ions in aqueous solution

principles.statement: Arrhenius acids and bases must be soluble electrolytes that dissociate completely or partially in water to release $\text{H}^+$ or $\text{OH}^-$
principles.application: Explains why HCl(aq) conducts electricity and neutralizes NaOH(aq), but HCl(g) does not exhibit acidic properties without water
principles.analogy: Like a key that only works in one lock—Arrhenius acids/bases only function in water, their specific 'solvent lock'

image.alt: HCl gas vs aqueous HCl comparison
image.caption: HCl exhibits acidic properties only when dissolved in water per Arrhenius theory

principles.statement: Arrhenius neutralization is the reaction $\text{H}^+_{(aq)} + \text{OH}^-_{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$ with $\Delta H^\circ = -57.1 \text{ kJ/mol}$
principles.application: Predicts that all strong acid-strong base neutralizations have the same net ionic equation and enthalpy change
principles.example: HCl + NaOH, HNO₃ + KOH, and H₂SO₄ + 2NaOH all reduce to $\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$
principles.analogy: Like different delivery trucks bringing the same package—the specific acid/base don't matter, only the $\text{H}^+$ and $\text{OH}^-$ ions

components.definition: The Arrhenius framework identifies acids and bases by their dissociation products in water
components.interpretation: The theory successfully explains aqueous conductivity, neutralization stoichiometry, and pH trends for common acids/bases, but cannot address non-aqueous systems or proton transfer without dissociation

image.caption: Arrhenius classification depends on dissociation products in water

classifications.value: Completely dissociate in water: HCl, HBr, HI, HNO₃, HClO₄, H₂SO₄ (first proton)
classifications.behavior: These acids exist entirely as $\text{H}^+$ and anion in solution; no undissociated HA remains

image.caption: Strong acids dissociate completely in water

classifications.value: Partially dissociate in water: CH₃COOH, HF, HCN, H₂CO₃

image.caption: Weak acids establish equilibrium with mostly undissociated molecules

classifications.value: Metal hydroxides that dissociate to release OH⁻: NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)₂, NH₃ (indirectly)
classifications.behavior: Strong bases (Group 1 hydroxides) dissociate completely; weak bases (NH₃) generate OH⁻ via reaction with water

image.caption: Strong bases dissociate completely to release hydroxide ions

examples.explanation: $\text{NH}_{3(aq)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_4^+_{(aq)} + \text{OH}^-_{(aq)}$; generates OH⁻ without containing OH in formula

image.caption: Ammonia produces OH⁻ by reacting with water, stretching Arrhenius definition

worked_examples.title: Identifying Arrhenius Acids and Bases
worked_examples.problem: Classify each substance as an Arrhenius acid, Arrhenius base, or neither: (a) HNO₃(aq), (b) CH₃OH(aq), (c) Ca(OH)₂(aq), (d) NH₃(aq), (e) CO₂(aq).

step_by_step.explanation: Recall Arrhenius definition: acids produce H⁺ in water; bases produce OH⁻ in water
Analyze HNO₃(aq): nitric acid dissociates completely
Analyze CH₃OH(aq): methanol does not dissociate to H⁺ or OH⁻
step_by_step.calculation: CH₃OH remains molecular in water; H is covalently bound, not ionic ✗ Neither

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze Ca(OH)₂(aq): calcium hydroxide dissociates to release OH⁻
Analyze NH₃(aq): ammonia reacts with water to generate OH⁻
Analyze CO₂(aq): carbon dioxide forms carbonic acid, which dissociates

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Acid; (b) Neither; (c) Base; (d) Base (indirect); (e) Acid (indirect). Note: NH₃ and CO₂ stretch the strict Arrhenius definition, motivating the Brønsted-Lowry theory.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Arrhenius classification based on dissociation products in water

image.caption: Arrhenius theory works well for ionic compounds but struggles with molecular acids/bases

sections.introduction: Developed independently by Johannes Brønsted and Thomas Lowry in 1923, this theory defines acids and bases by proton ($\text{H}^+$) transfer rather than dissociation products. This broader framework explains acid-base behavior in any solvent and introduces the crucial concept of conjugate pairs.
sections.key_concept: Brønsted-Lowry Acid: A proton ($\text{H}^+$) donor; Brønsted-Lowry Base: A proton ($\text{H}^+$) acceptor. Every acid-base reaction involves two conjugate pairs: $\text{HA} + \text{B} \rightleftharpoons \text{A}^- + \text{BH}^+$

categories.description: Species that differ by exactly one proton ($\text{H}^+$); the conjugate base has one fewer H and one more negative charge than its acid
categories.detailed_mechanism: When an acid donates a proton, it becomes its conjugate base; when a base accepts a proton, it becomes its conjugate acid. The strength relationship is inverse: strong acid → weak conjugate base; weak acid → strong conjugate base

examples.explanation: $\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{H}^+ + \text{Cl}^-$; HCl is strong acid ($K_a \approx 10^7$), so Cl⁻ is negligible base ($K_b \approx 10^{-21}$)

image.caption: Strong acids have negligible conjugate bases
Weak acids have relatively strong conjugate bases

categories.description: Substances that can act as either Brønsted-Lowry acids or bases depending on reaction partner
categories.detailed_mechanism: Amphoteric species contain both a removable proton and a lone pair to accept a proton; common examples include H₂O, HCO₃⁻, HSO₄⁻, and amino acids

examples.explanation: $\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_3\text{O}^+ + \text{OH}^-$; one water acts as acid (donates H⁺), the other as base (accepts H⁺)

image.alt: Water acting as both acid and base
image.caption: Water is amphoteric: can donate or accept protons

image.alt: Bicarbonate acting as acid or base
image.caption: Bicarbonate ion is amphoteric, key to blood buffering

categories.description: Brønsted-Lowry theory explains why acid/base strength depends on the solvent's ability to accept or donate protons
categories.detailed_mechanism: In a basic solvent (e.g., NH₃), even weak acids appear strong because the solvent readily accepts protons; in acidic solvents (e.g., acetic acid), even weak bases appear strong

examples.process: HCl in water vs. acetic acid
examples.explanation: In water: HCl is strong acid (water accepts H⁺ readily); in glacial acetic acid: HCl is weak acid (acetic acid is poorer proton acceptor than water)

image.caption: Acid strength depends on solvent proton-accepting ability

process_flow.description: In the reaction, determine which species loses H⁺ (acid) and which gains H⁺ (base)
process_flow.mechanism: Look for species with removable H (acid) and species with lone pairs or negative charge (base)
process_flow.result: Labeled acid and base on reactant side

process_flow.description: The acid's product (after losing H⁺) is its conjugate base; the base's product (after gaining H⁺) is its conjugate acid
process_flow.mechanism: Remove H⁺ from acid to get conjugate base; add H⁺ to base to get conjugate acid; adjust charges accordingly
process_flow.result: Two conjugate pairs: acid/conjugate base and base/conjugate acid

process_flow.description: Equilibrium favors the side with the weaker acid and weaker base
process_flow.mechanism: Compare $K_a$ values: reaction proceeds from stronger acid + stronger base → weaker acid + weaker base
process_flow.result: Prediction of whether equilibrium lies left or right

image.caption: Three-step process for Brønsted-Lowry acid-base analysis

worked_examples.title: Analyzing a Brønsted-Lowry Reaction
worked_examples.problem: For the reaction $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}_{(aq)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-_{(aq)} + \text{H}_3\text{O}^+_{(aq)}$, identify the acid, base, conjugate acid, and conjugate base. Predict the direction of equilibrium.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify proton transfer: which species loses H⁺ and which gains it?
step_by_step.calculation: CH₃COOH loses H⁺ → becomes CH₃COO⁻ (acid); H₂O gains H⁺ → becomes H₃O⁺ (base)
Acid: CH₃COOH; Conjugate base: CH₃COO⁻; Base: H₂O; Conjugate acid: H₃O⁺

step_by_step.explanation: Compare acid strengths to predict equilibrium direction
Apply principle: equilibrium favors weaker acid/base side
step_by_step.calculation: Since H₃O⁺ is stronger acid than CH₃COOH, equilibrium lies left (reactants favored); $K_{eq} = \frac{K_a(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH})}{K_a(\text{H}_3\text{O}^+)} \approx 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$

worked_examples.final_answer: Acid: CH₃COOH; Base: H₂O; Conjugate base: CH₃COO⁻; Conjugate acid: H₃O⁺; Equilibrium favors reactants (left) because H₃O⁺ is a stronger acid than CH₃COOH
worked_examples.concept_applied: Brønsted-Lowry conjugate pairs and equilibrium prediction using relative acid strengths

image.caption: Acetic acid is a weak acid: equilibrium lies toward reactants

sections.introduction: Gilbert N. Lewis proposed the most general acid-base theory in 1923, defining acids and bases by electron-pair transfer rather than proton transfer. This framework encompasses all Brønsted-Lowry reactions plus many others, including coordination chemistry and organic reactions.

key_concept.definition: Lewis Acid: An electron-pair acceptor (electrophile); Lewis Base: An electron-pair donor (nucleophile)
key_concept.context: This definition includes reactions without protons, such as $\text{BF}_3 + \text{:NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{F}_3\text{B:NH}_3$, expanding acid-base chemistry to coordination complexes and organic mechanisms

image.alt: Electron-pair donation from NH3 to BF3
image.caption: Lewis acid-base reaction: base donates electron pair to acid's empty orbital

principles.statement: Lewis base donates a lone pair to form a coordinate covalent bond with Lewis acid's empty orbital
principles.application: Explains formation of complex ions, catalysis by metal ions, and organic reactions like Friedel-Crafts
principles.example: $\text{Ag}^+ + 2\text{:NH}_3 \rightarrow [\text{H}_3\text{N:Ag:NH}_3]^+$; Ag⁺ (acid) accepts electron pairs from NH₃ (base)
principles.analogy: Like a handshake—the base offers its electron 'hand' to the acid's empty 'handshake slot'

image.caption: Coordinate bond: both electrons come from the Lewis base

principles.statement: Lewis acids typically have: (1) incomplete octet (BF₃), (2) polar multiple bonds (CO₂), (3) empty d-orbitals (metal ions), or (4) ability to expand octet (SiF₄)
principles.application: Predicts which species can act as Lewis acids in synthesis and catalysis
principles.example: BF₃ has only 6 valence electrons on B; accepts lone pair from :NH₃ to complete octet
principles.analogy: Like an incomplete puzzle—Lewis acids have 'missing pieces' (electron deficiency) that bases can fill

principles.statement: Every Brønsted-Lowry acid-base reaction is also a Lewis reaction: H⁺ (Lewis acid) accepts electron pair from base
principles.application: Unifies proton transfer and electron-pair transfer under one conceptual framework
principles.example: $\text{HCl} + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{NH}_4^+ + \text{Cl}^-$: H⁺ (Lewis acid) accepts lone pair from NH₃ (Lewis base); Cl⁻ is spectator
principles.analogy: Like Russian nesting dolls—Lewis theory contains Brønsted-Lowry, which contains Arrhenius

components.definition: Lewis theory identifies acids and bases by their electron-pair accepting/donating behavior
components.equation: General reaction: $\text{A} + \text{:B} \rightarrow \text{A:B}$ where A = Lewis acid (electron acceptor), :B = Lewis base (electron donor)
components.interpretation: This framework explains coordination chemistry, catalysis, and organic reaction mechanisms that proton-transfer theories cannot address

image.alt: Lewis acid and base structural features
image.caption: Lewis acids have electron-deficient centers; Lewis bases have lone pairs

classifications.value: Electron-deficient species: BF₃, AlCl₃, Fe³⁺, H⁺, SO₃, CO₂, carbocations
characteristics: Accept electron pair to form bond
classifications.behavior: Lewis acids seek electron density; reactivity increases with greater electron deficiency and lower steric hindrance

examples.process: BF₃ as Lewis acid in ether
examples.explanation: $\text{BF}_3 + \text{:O(CH}_2\text{CH}_3)_2 \rightarrow \text{F}_3\text{B:O(CH}_2\text{CH}_3)_2$; boron's empty p-orbital accepts oxygen's lone pair

image.caption: BF₃ accepts electron pair from ether oxygen

examples.process: Fe³⁺ as Lewis acid in water
examples.explanation: $\text{Fe}^{3+} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow [\text{Fe(H}_2\text{O})_6]^{3+}$; metal ion accepts lone pairs from six water molecules

image.caption: Metal ions act as Lewis acids by accepting electron pairs from ligands

classifications.value: Electron-rich species: NH₃, H₂O, OH⁻, CN⁻, :PR₃, alkenes, aromatic rings
characteristics: Donate electron pair to form bond
classifications.behavior: Lewis base strength depends on electron density, polarizability, and steric accessibility of the donor site

examples.explanation: NH₃ donates lone pair on nitrogen to Lewis acids: $\text{:NH}_3 + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{NH}_4^+$; $\text{:NH}_3 + \text{BF}_3 \rightarrow \text{H}_3\text{N:BF}_3$

image.caption: Ammonia's lone pair on nitrogen makes it a versatile Lewis base

examples.explanation: π-electrons in C=C can donate to Lewis acids: $\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2^+$ (electrophilic addition)

image.caption: π-bonds can act as Lewis bases by donating electron density

classifications.value: Species that can act as either Lewis acid or base depending on reaction partner
characteristics: Have both lone pairs and empty orbitals
classifications.behavior: Ambident species like CO, NO, or transition metal complexes can switch roles based on the electronic demands of the reaction

examples.explanation: CO can donate via C lone pair (σ-donor) or accept into π* orbital (π-acceptor); in metal carbonyls, both interactions occur simultaneously

image.caption: CO acts as both Lewis base (σ-donation) and Lewis acid (π-backbonding)

worked_examples.title: Identifying Lewis Acids and Bases
worked_examples.problem: For each reaction, identify the Lewis acid and Lewis base: (a) $\text{AlCl}_3 + \text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{AlCl}_4^-$, (b) $\text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, (c) $\text{Zn}^{2+} + 4\text{CN}^- \rightarrow [\text{Zn(CN)}_4]^{2-}$.

step_by_step.explanation: Recall: Lewis acid accepts electron pair; Lewis base donates electron pair

step_by_step.calculation: Al in AlCl₃ has only 6 valence electrons (electron-deficient); Cl⁻ has lone pairs; Cl⁻ donates to Al: Lewis acid = AlCl₃, Lewis base = Cl⁻
S in SO₃ is electron-deficient (polar S=O bonds); O in H₂O has lone pairs; H₂O donates to S: Lewis acid = SO₃, Lewis base = H₂O
Zn²⁺ has empty orbitals; CN⁻ has lone pair on C; each CN⁻ donates to Zn²⁺: Lewis acid = Zn²⁺, Lewis base = CN⁻
In each case, electron pair moves from base to acid, forming a coordinate covalent bond ✓

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Acid: AlCl₃, Base: Cl⁻; (b) Acid: SO₃, Base: H₂O; (c) Acid: Zn²⁺, Base: CN⁻. All involve formation of coordinate covalent bonds via electron-pair donation.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Lewis acid-base identification by electron-pair donation/acceptance

image.caption: Lewis theory explains diverse reactions through electron-pair transfer

sections.section_title: Comparing and Applying the Three Theories
sections.introduction: Each acid-base theory has strengths and limitations. Understanding when to apply each framework enables accurate prediction and explanation of chemical behavior across diverse contexts.

key_concept.definition: Theory Selection Guide: Use Arrhenius for aqueous electrolyte behavior; Brønsted-Lowry for proton transfer in any solvent; Lewis for electron-pair transfer including coordination and organic reactions
key_concept.context: The theories are hierarchical: Lewis ⊃ Brønsted-Lowry ⊃ Arrhenius; choose the simplest theory that explains the observed behavior

image.caption: Select the appropriate theory based on reaction type and context

categories.description: Best for: aqueous solutions of ionic acids/bases, conductivity predictions, simple neutralization stoichiometry
categories.detailed_mechanism: Arrhenius theory excels when species dissociate into H⁺ or OH⁻ in water; fails for non-aqueous systems, molecular acids without dissociation, or reactions without proton transfer

examples.explanation: Arrhenius theory perfectly describes: $\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$; predicts 1:1 stoichiometry and conductivity changes

image.caption: Arrhenius theory works well for aqueous strong acid-base titrations

categories.description: Best for: proton transfer reactions in any solvent, conjugate pair analysis, predicting equilibrium direction, buffer systems
categories.detailed_mechanism: Brønsted-Lowry theory explains acid strength trends via conjugate base stability, solvent effects on dissociation, and amphoteric behavior

examples.explanation: $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{HCO}_3^-$; adding acid shifts equilibrium left; adding base shifts right; Brønsted-Lowry conjugate pairs explain pH stability

image.caption: Brønsted-Lowry theory explains buffer action via conjugate pairs

categories.description: Best for: coordination chemistry, organic reaction mechanisms, catalysis, reactions without protons, explaining hard/soft acid-base behavior
categories.detailed_mechanism: Lewis theory encompasses all electron-pair transfer reactions, explaining complex formation, electrophilic/nucleophilic reactions, and catalytic cycles

examples.explanation: AlCl₃ (Lewis acid) accepts electron pair from Cl⁻ in R-Cl, generating carbocation electrophile that attacks aromatic ring (Lewis base)

image.caption: Lewis theory explains catalysis and organic reaction mechanisms

worked_examples.title: Choosing the Right Theory: NH₃ Reactions
worked_examples.problem: Ammonia participates in three reactions: (a) NH₃(aq) + H₂O ⇌ NH₄⁺ + OH⁻, (b) NH₃(g) + HCl(g) → NH₄Cl(s), (c) NH₃ + BF₃ → H₃N-BF₃. Which acid-base theory best explains each?

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze reaction (a): NH₃(aq) + H₂O ⇌ NH₄⁺ + OH⁻
step_by_step.calculation: Proton transfer in water: NH₃ accepts H⁺ from H₂O. Brønsted-Lowry explains conjugate pairs (NH₄⁺/NH₃, H₂O/OH⁻) and equilibrium. Arrhenius works indirectly (OH⁻ production). Lewis also applies (NH₃ donates to H⁺). Best: Brønsted-Lowry.
Gas-phase proton transfer: NH₃ accepts H⁺ from HCl. Arrhenius fails (no water). Brønsted-Lowry explains proton transfer. Lewis also applies. Best: Brønsted-Lowry (simplest adequate theory).
No proton transfer: NH₃ donates lone pair to electron-deficient B in BF₃. Arrhenius and Brønsted-Lowry fail (no H⁺ involved). Lewis theory explains electron-pair donation. Best: Lewis theory.
(a) Brønsted-Lowry; (b) Brønsted-Lowry; (c) Lewis. Choose the simplest theory that fully explains the reaction mechanism.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Brønsted-Lowry (proton transfer in water); (b) Brønsted-Lowry (gas-phase proton transfer); (c) Lewis (electron-pair donation without protons). Theory choice depends on reaction mechanism, not just reactants.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Selecting appropriate acid-base theory based on reaction type and context

image.alt: Ammonia reactions explained by different theories
image.caption: Different ammonia reactions require different theoretical frameworks

summary: Acid-base chemistry is described by three progressively broader theories: Arrhenius (1884) defines acids as H⁺ producers and bases as OH⁻ producers in water; Brønsted-Lowry (1923) defines acids as proton donors and bases as proton acceptors in any solvent, introducing conjugate pairs and explaining equilibrium direction; Lewis (1923) defines acids as electron-pair acceptors and bases as electron-pair donors, encompassing all proton-transfer reactions plus coordination chemistry and organic mechanisms. Each theory includes the previous while expanding applicability: Arrhenius explains aqueous electrolyte behavior; Brønsted-Lowry explains proton transfer, buffer action, and solvent effects; Lewis explains complex formation, catalysis, and reactions without protons. Key concepts include conjugate pairs (species differing by H⁺), amphoteric species (can act as acid or base), and the inverse relationship between acid strength and conjugate base strength. Selecting the appropriate theory depends on reaction context: use Arrhenius for aqueous dissociation, Brønsted-Lowry for proton transfer equilibria, and Lewis for electron-pair transfer mechanisms. Mastery of these frameworks enables prediction of acid-base behavior across aqueous solutions, non-aqueous solvents, coordination complexes, and organic reaction mechanisms.
